penaltyDetail.Note: Clean licence entering 2026 — 0 active superlicense points

George Russell.bio: George Russell joined Mercedes in 2022 after three seasons proving himself at Williams — often extracting top-10 finishes from a backmarker car. His first win came at the 2022 Brazilian GP when he subbed for Hamilton at Mercedes. By 2025 he had established himself as team leader, winning in Canada and Singapore en route to a career-high 9 podiums. The 2026 season, with its new active-aero regulations favouring Mercedes' approach, looks like his best shot at a maiden title.

Kimi Antonelli.bio: At 19 years and 201 days, Kimi Antonelli became the youngest ever F1 GP pole-sitter at the 2026 Chinese GP — beating Vettel's 2008 record — then won the race and set the fastest lap to complete a hat-trick, becoming the second youngest GP winner in history (behind Verstappen's 2016 Spanish GP win at 18) and the first Italian winner since Fisichella at the 2006 Malaysian GP. In FP3 at the AUS season opener, Antonelli had a heavy crash that almost prevented him from qualifying — he recovered to qualify P2 and finish P2. Spotted by Mercedes in karting, he skipped Formula 3. His 2025 debut included a crash in FP1 at Monza but he still scored three podiums and a Sprint pole at Miami. His ceiling in F1 looks limitless.

seasons.note: Youngest ever GP pole-sitter; first F1 win China; FP3 crash in AUS
7th in WDC on debut; 3 podiums; infamous Monza practice crash

Lewis Hamilton.bio: The most decorated driver in Formula 1 history with 7 World Championships and 105 wins. Hamilton joined Ferrari for 2026 in a move that shocked the world. His first Ferrari podium came in China (Race 2 of 2026), launching what many hope will be a final championship chapter. Dominant at Mercedes from 2014–2020, he lost the 2021 title in controversial circumstances at Abu Dhabi. A generational talent whose statistical legacy may never be surpassed.

Charles Leclerc.bio: Charles Leclerc has been Ferrari's most naturally gifted driver since Schumacher. He became youngest Ferrari race winner and set a record 9 poles in 2022. Consistently fast in qualifying — the front row is his natural habitat. However, poor Ferrari strategy and reliability have denied him a title multiple times. With Hamilton now as teammate, 2026 will test whether he can lead Ferrari to its first constructors' title since 2008.

Max Verstappen.bio: Four consecutive World Championships (2021–2024) put Verstappen in conversation with the all-time greats. His 2023 season — 19 wins from 22 races — is statistically the greatest single season in F1 history. After losing the 2025 title to Norris by just 2 points, Verstappen switched his permanent number from #33 to #3 for 2026. He scored 8 points from P6 in Australia despite qualifying P20 after a crash, then DNS in China with Red Bull Ford PU reliability issues. Racing #3 for the first time.

penaltyDetail.Note: Clean licence entering 2026 — 0 active superlicense points

Lando Norris.bio: 2025 World Champion and now racing with the iconic #1 on his McLaren. Norris finally converted his undeniable speed into a title in a three-way battle with Verstappen and Piastri that went to the final lap of the season in Abu Dhabi. Famous for his raw pace and social media presence, he arrived at McLaren in 2019 as a 19-year-old and never left. His first win came at Miami 2024. In 2026 McLaren have suffered car-issue DNS failures in China — he sits P6 with 15 points after two rounds, a stunning fall from champion to midfield.

seasons.note: P6 AUS / DNS China (car issue on grid)

Oscar Piastri.bio: Oscar Piastri arrived at McLaren in 2023 after Alpine controversially attempted to place him at Williams — the resulting contract dispute forced him to McLaren. He repaid their faith emphatically, winning his first race at Budapest 2024. In 2025 he was championship contender before narrowly losing out to Norris. In 2026 he suffered a DNS in China alongside Norris — the two cars had separate electrical issues with the Mercedes PU integration — leaving McLaren 80 points behind Mercedes after just 2 rounds. Both drivers had started in Australia.

seasons.note: P9 AUS / DNS China (car issue) — no race laps in CHN

Carlos Sainz.bio: Carlos Sainz is the ultimate professional — consistent, fast and a fierce qualifier. His four wins include a stunning 2024 Australian GP victory before Ferrari replaced him with Hamilton. Sainz moved to Williams for 2025 and immediately helped them achieve their best constructors' finish since 2017. Known for his 'El Plan' methodical approach and fierce determination, he remains one of the best drivers never to have won a championship.

penaltyDetail.Note: Clean licence entering 2026 — 0 active superlicense points

Fernando Alonso.bio: The only two-time champion still racing. Fernando Alonso is a living legend — his duels with Schumacher, Hamilton and Vettel define an era of F1. He returned from a sabbatical in 2021 and has driven at a level that humiliates drivers half his age. Joined Aston Martin in 2023 but the Adrian Newey-era 2026 technical revolution has left Aston Martin completely off the pace. At 44, time may finally be catching up with one of the greatest racers who ever lived.

penaltyDetail.Note: 0 active points — not on grid in 2025; points expired

Valtteri Bottas.bio: Valtteri Bottas served six seasons as Hamilton's wingman at Mercedes, accumulating 10 wins and 67 podiums but never a championship. Moved to Alfa Romeo/Sauber (2022–2024) where he scored regularly despite lesser machinery. Left on the shelf for 2025, he was signed by Cadillac for their F1 debut in 2026. At 36 and with an unbeatable contract with a new team, Bottas brings invaluable experience to help build a constructor from scratch.

penaltyDetail.Note: 0 active points — not on grid in 2025; all 2024 points expired

Sergio P\u00e9rez.bio: Known as 'The Minister of Defence', Sergio Pérez is one of the best tyre managers in F1 history. His six wins include back-to-back Monaco/Azerbaijan doubles. Six seasons at Force India/Racing Point established him as a midfield master before Red Bull signed him for 2021. His tenure ended acrimoniously in 2024 after a poor second half of the season. Now at Cadillac with Bottas, he is determined to restore his reputation on the global stage.

Pierre Gasly.bio: Pierre Gasly's career has been a rollercoaster. Promoted to Red Bull in 2019 then demoted back to Toro Rosso after a poor start — he responded by winning at Monza 2020 in one of F1's most emotional victories. The Frenchman moved to Alpha Tauri and then Alpine in 2023, where he has been the team's sole points scorer. Now benefiting from Mercedes power in 2026, Alpine hope their new PU will unlock the speed Gasly clearly possesses.

Liam Lawson.bio: Liam Lawson showed real grit in his substitute stints at Red Bull's junior team in 2023 and 2024 before getting a full-time seat at Racing Bulls in 2025. Passed over for a Red Bull promotion in favour of Hadjar despite performing well, he has channelled that disappointment into focused performances. Eighth in the 2026 standings after two races, matching Verstappen's points haul, is remarkable given the resources of each team.

Oliver Bearman.bio: Oliver Bearman burst onto the scene with a stunning P7 on debut for Ferrari (subbing for Sainz at the 2024 Saudi Arabian GP) at just 18. He later subbed for Haas at Baku before earning a full-time seat there in 2025 — where he outscored teammate Ocon and showed raw race craft that excited Ferrari enough to retain his Academy ties. His fifth place in the 2026 standings after two races is the talk of the paddock.

Alexander Albon.bio: Alexander Albon had a promising Red Bull career cut short but rebuilt it patiently at Williams. By 2025 he had become the team's undisputed leader, finishing fifth in the constructors' standings alongside Sainz. Known for his ability to nurse tyres and extract unexpected results from difficult strategy positions, he is a fierce midfield racer who brings positive team culture wherever he goes.

penaltyDetail.Note: Clean licence entering 2026 — 2024 points fully expired

Nico H\u00fclkenberg.bio: 'The Hulk' is the most experienced driver on the grid never to have stood on the podium — until his landmark first podium at the 2025 British GP. Hülkenberg was out of F1 entirely between 2019 and 2022 before Aston Martin and then Haas revived his career. Now at Audi for their works debut, he brings essential experience to help develop their new power unit through the inevitable teething troubles.

Esteban Ocon.bio: Esteban Ocon won the 2021 Hungarian GP in chaotic conditions — his only F1 victory. After five seasons at Alpine (where he and Alonso famously clashed) he moved to Haas in 2025 but was comprehensively outperformed by rookie Bearman. Now in his second Haas season, he needs to reassert himself before his seat comes under further pressure.

Lance Stroll.bio: Lance Stroll drives for his father's team and has survived sustained criticism to remain in F1 for nine seasons. He has shown genuine speed on occasion — a stunning pole at Istanbul 2020 and two wet-weather podiums highlight his capability in atypical conditions. However, his consistency remains well below his illustrious teammates and the team's technical talent. 2026 with its new regulations has left Aston Martin completely off the pace.

Yuki Tsunoda.bio: Yuki Tsunoda is the first Japanese driver to race in F1 since Kamui Kobayashi. Fiery on radio and rapid on track — he has matured considerably since his crash-heavy rookie season in 2021. Moved to Red Bull for 2026 replacing Hadjar but has yet to score points as Red Bull Ford Powertrains DM01 reliability issues have hampered the team. Notably, he now races with the Red Bull Ford PU rather than Honda — with whom he shared a nationality — as Honda switched exclusively to power Aston Martin in 2026.

Gabriel Bortoleto.bio: Gabriel Bortoleto won the 2024 Formula 2 championship as McLaren junior driver, then was controversially loaned to Audi for 2025 where he performed well in a backmarker. The 2026 Audi — with a new works PU — has continued to struggle but Bortoleto is regarded as one of the highest-ceiling young talents in the sport. A future McLaren seat is the goal.

Franco Colapinto.bio: Franco Colapinto burst into F1 mid-2024 at Williams as a Sargeant replacement and immediately impressed — scoring points in his debut races. Moved to Alpine for 2025 mid-season, replacing Doohan, he brings Latin flair to a French team. Argentina's first F1 driver in years, his passionate fanbase is enormous. His 2026 campaign at Alpine with Mercedes power will define whether his early promise translates into a long-term seat.

penaltyDetail.Note: Clean licence entering 2026 — 0 active superlicense points

Isack Hadjar.bio: Isack Hadjar spent 2025 at Racing Bulls after winning the 2024 F2 title, gaining podium experience at Zandvoort. Promoted to Red Bull for 2026 — arguably the biggest opportunity in F1 for a 20-year-old. His 4 points from the opening two rounds (vs Verstappen's 8 from better fortune) suggest he is not overawed by the occasion. The French-Algerian driver is widely considered the brightest talent from the junior ranks.

seasons.note: Debut at Red Bull — 4pts from two races

Arvid Lindblad.bio: Arvid Lindblad is 2026's only true rookie — jumping straight from Formula 2 (which he hadn't even completed) to F1 at 18 years old. Red Bull Academy's biggest bet. The British-Swedish driver is described by Red Bull's youth programme as having the highest natural talent they've seen since Max Verstappen. His 4 points from the first two races, matching veterans in faster machinery, hint that the hype may be entirely justified.

seasons.note: Debut at Racing Bulls — equalling Red Bull's tally

Mercedes.bio: The most successful constructor of the modern hybrid era, Mercedes won eight consecutive constructors' championships from 2014 to 2021. After a tough transition to the ground-effect regulations in 2022, they rebuilt methodically, reclaiming the championship in 2026 with a dominant mastery of the new active-aerodynamic regulations. Their W17 has been the benchmark from race one, with Russell and Antonelli securing back-to-back 1-2 finishes in Australia and China.

seasons.note: P2 — Russell P4 WDC (319pts), Antonelli 7th (150pts) on debut
Russell and Hamilton both winners; new car concept finally clicked mid-season
Lost to Red Bull in Abu Dhabi

Ferrari.bio: The most storied constructor in Formula 1 history. Ferrari have competed in every World Championship since 1950 and hold records for most race wins, podiums and pole positions. Their last constructors' title came in 2008. The signing of Lewis Hamilton for 2026 was seismic — his first Ferrari podium at the Chinese GP was an emotional moment for the sport. With Hamilton and Leclerc, Ferrari have arguably their strongest driver pairing in a generation.
notable: Hamilton joined 2026 — first podium at Chinese GP

seasons.note: P4 — Leclerc 5th WDC (242pts), Hamilton 6th (156pts) in Ferrari debut
P3 — Leclerc and Sainz both race winners; strategy errors remain costly
Vettel title challenge closest since 2012; Germany error proved decisive

McLaren.bio: Reigning 2025 World Champions after Lando Norris claimed McLaren's first drivers' title since 2008. Their 2025 MCL59 was the fastest car on the grid. The 2026 season has been a disaster early on — both Norris and Piastri failed to start the Chinese GP due to separate electrical issues with the Mercedes PU integration. Bortoleto (Audi) and Albon (Williams) also DNS'd the same race, suggesting a broader issue with specific grid conditions. McLaren trail Mercedes by 80 points after two rounds, an almost insurmountable gap if not arrested immediately.
notable: Norris races #1 in 2026 as reigning champion
CHN R2: Norris & Piastri DNS — car issues

seasons.note: Car issues DNS — Norris/Piastri (+ Bortoleto/Albon) R2
🏆 Back-to-back WCC — Norris WDC by 2pts over Verstappen in Abu Dhabi
🏆 First WCC since 1998 — ended 26-year drought; Norris first win Miami
P5 — Rebuilding phase; Norris consistent scorer; new chassis concept bedding in

Red Bull.bio: Red Bull Racing dominated F1 from 2010–2013 and again from 2022–2024, the latter stretch including Verstappen's record-breaking 2023 season of 19 victories. For 2026 they ended their Honda partnership and launched Red Bull Ford Powertrains — a fully in-house engine built with Ford backing. The DM01 power unit has suffered catastrophic reliability in the opening two races, leaving Verstappen pointless and the team in crisis mode.
notable: Verstappen switches to #3 in 2026 — first driver to change permanent number
2026: RBF DM01 PU reliability crisis — Verstappen DNS CHN

seasons.note: P3 — Verstappen 8 wins but lost WDC by 2pts; Tsunoda weak second driver

Racing Bulls.fullName: Visa Cash App Racing Bulls F1 Team
Racing Bulls.bio: One of F1's oldest continuous midfield outfits, operating under many names — Minardi, Toro Rosso, AlphaTauri and now Racing Bulls. Their sole victory remains Gasly's stunning win at Monza 2020. As Red Bull's junior team they have launched the careers of Vettel, Verstappen, Ricciardo, Sainz, Gasly and now Lawson and Lindblad. In 2026 Lindblad — just 18 — is matching Verstappen's points tally despite racing for the junior team.
notable: Gasly win at Monza 2020 — sole victory
Launch pad for Vettel, Verstappen, Ricciardo
Lindblad 2026 — youngest driver of the season
Outscoring senior Red Bull team in 2026

seasons.note: P6 — Hadjar Zandvoort podium; Lawson/Tsunoda swapped with Red Bull at R3
Tsunoda lead driver; Ricciardo replaced mid-season by Lawson
Tsunoda lead driver; de Vries replaced by Ricciardo mid-year
Still as AlphaTauri; Gasly final year before Alpine switch

Haas.bio: The only American constructor on the current F1 grid, Haas entered in 2016 and immediately scored points on debut. As Ferrari customers they run the same power unit as Scuderia Ferrari. Oliver Bearman — on loan from the Ferrari Driver Academy — has been their standout in 2026, delivering performances that have stunned the paddock. Their P4 in the constructors' standings after two races is almost entirely down to Bearman's brilliance.
notable: TGR (Toyota) title sponsor from 2026 — Ferrari PU continues
Hülkenberg's first career podium in 2025 (British GP)

seasons.note: P8 — Bearman standout rookie; Hulkenberg British GP podium his career first
Hulkenberg led; Magnussen race ban then dropped for Bearman at year-end
Hulkenberg return season; Magnussen race banned for Monza move on Alonso
Strong start then Mazepin dropped for Russia sanctions; Magnussen recalled
Grosjean and Magnussen scored but team politics beginning to surface

Alpine.bio: Formerly Renault, Lotus, Toleman and Benetton — Alpine's Enstone base has produced two World Champions (Schumacher with Benetton in 1994-95, Alonso with Renault in 2005-06). After years running their own Renault PU they switched to Mercedes customer power for 2026, a pragmatic decision following the end of the Renault F1 engine programme. Gasly has been their star in 2026, while Colapinto brings Argentinian passion and raw speed.
notable: Enstone factory one of F1's most storied bases

seasons.note: P10 — Worst season in years; Renault PU farewell; Doohan replaced by Colapinto mid-season
Gasly settled after Alpine move; Ocon consistent but Renault PU falling behind

Williams.bio: The most successful privateer team in F1 history with 7 constructors' championships and 114 race wins. Their golden era was 1986-1997 under Frank Williams and Patrick Head. After years in the midfield and back of the grid, James Vowles (ex-Mercedes strategist) has rebuilt the team methodically. Carlos Sainz joining in 2025 gave them a proven frontrunner. In 2026 they're a competitive midfield presence, though still some way from their championship-winning days.
notable: Senna, Mansell, Prost, Hill, Villeneuve all drove for Williams
Sainz 2025 win — first Williams win since 2004

seasons.note: P5 — Best result since 2003; Sainz P3 Baku and Qatar; Albon P8 in WDC
Albon scored both points; Colapinto mid-season debut impressed the paddock

Aston Martin.bio: Racing Point rebranded as Aston Martin for 2021 under the ownership of Lawrence Stroll. Their 2023 season — driven by Fernando Alonso — was a revelation, with 8 podiums and 4th in the constructors' standings. The signing of Adrian Newey as CTO in 2024 was the biggest technical hire in F1 in years. For 2026 they are the exclusive works Honda HRC partner — a completely new and independent power unit programme. However, the 2026 regulations have reset their competitive position entirely and they are yet to score.

seasons.note: P7 — Newey arrived as CTO mid-season; Alonso P5 Saudi GP best result

Audi.bio: The Hinwil-based constructor that began as Sauber — the team that launched Michael Schumacher's career on loan in 1991 — is now the works Audi F1 factory team for 2026. Their sole victory came as BMW Sauber at the 2008 Canadian GP with Robert Kubica. Mattia Binotto (ex-Ferrari team principal) leads a major rebranding and technical rebuild. Gabriel Bortoleto and Nico Hülkenberg are tasked with developing the brand-new Audi power unit through its inevitable teething pains.
notable: Kubica win Canadian GP 2008 — sole victory (as BMW Sauber)
Audi took over for 2026 works programme
Brand new Audi PU — first works entry

seasons.note: P9 — Final Sauber era; Hulkenberg 51pts led team; Bortoleto rookie impressed
Bottas and Zhou final year; Binotto appointed incoming team principal

Cadillac.bio: The 11th team on the 2026 grid, Cadillac (backed by General Motors) entered F1 after years of effort by Michael Andretti's organisation to gain approval from the FOM. They run Ferrari customer power and have recruited Valtteri Bottas and Sergio Pérez — both with extensive F1 experience — to help build the constructor from scratch. As a debut team, points are not expected immediately but Bottas and Pérez have the racecraft to capitalise on chaotic conditions.
notable: Michael Andretti's years-long FOM battle to enter
Bottas (248 starts) and Pérez (283 starts) — most experienced debut lineup ever

AUS.name: Albert Park Circuit
AUS.bio: The Albert Park street circuit has hosted the Australian GP since 1996. Set in a public park around a lake in suburban Melbourne, its smooth asphalt and flowing fast corners — especially the sweeping Turns 11-13 complex — reward brave driving. The 2026 opener was the first race under the new active aero and power unit regulations. Russell started from pole and survived early pressure from Leclerc and Hamilton to lead Antonelli home in a Mercedes 1-2. Antonelli had crashed heavily in FP3, while Piastri's McLaren suffered an unexpected surge on the reconnaissance lap — both Piastri and Hülkenberg did not start. Bortoleto (P9) scored Audi's first-ever F1 points, and debutant Lindblad (P8) impressed for Racing Bulls.
AUS.weather: Mild and unpredictable — risk of rain
notable: First GP under active aero regs in 2026 — new era begins
Verstappen crashed in Q1 (rear axle locked under braking) — started P20, recovered to P6
Piastri DNS — McLaren suffered unexpected engine surge on reconnaissance lap
Bortoleto P9 — Audi's first-ever F1 championship points
Lindblad P8 debut — first Faenza-team debut points scorer since Tsunoda 2021 Bahrain
Schumacher comeback win 2004 · McLaren MP4/4 dominated 1988 season

CHN.name: Shanghai International Circuit
CHN.bio: Shanghai's sweeping back straight and the unique 'snail shell' Turn 1–2 complex make it one of F1's most technically demanding layouts. The 2026 race was the emotional high of the early season — Kimi Antonelli took pole, won, and set the fastest lap at just 19 — achieving a hat-trick and becoming the second youngest race winner in F1 history (behind Verstappen's 2016 Spanish GP win) and the first Italian winner since Fisichella at the 2006 Malaysian GP. Note: the hat-trick achievement stands even though fastest lap no longer scores a point since 2025. Hamilton stood on the podium in Ferrari red for the first time (P3), while Leclerc finished P4. Russell won the Sprint. Piastri, Norris, Bortoleto and Albon all DNS — car issues. Verstappen, Alonso and Stroll retired from the race.
CHN.tyreWear: High — abrasive surface and long corners load rear tyres significantly
notable: Antonelli hat-trick: pole + win + fastest lap (no bonus point since 2025)
Second youngest GP winner ever — behind Verstappen 2016 (18yr)
Youngest ever GP pole-sitter — beat Vettel's 2008 Italian GP record
First Italian GP winner since Fisichella 2006 Malaysian GP
Russell won Sprint · Hamilton first Ferrari podium (P3 race)
DNS: Piastri, Norris, Bortoleto, Albon · DNF: Verstappen, Alonso, Stroll

JPN.name: Suzuka International Racing Course
JPN.bio: Suzuka is widely regarded as the greatest racing circuit in the world. Its unique figure-eight layout creates a flowing sequence of high-speed corners — Esses, Dunlop, Degner, 130R — that punish the slightest error. It is one of only two crossover circuits in F1. Drivers consistently rank Suzuka as their favourite. Championships are frequently decided here, and the passionate Japanese fans create an electric atmosphere unlike any other venue.
JPN.tyreWear: Medium — high-speed nature loads front tyres through sustained lateral g
JPN.keyCorner: Spoon Curve · 130R (taken flat in qualifying) · Casino Triangle chicane
JPN.weather: Early spring — cool, risk of rain increases Saturday
notable: 1989/90 Senna vs Prost title collisions here

MIA.name: Miami International Autodrome
MIA.bio: Built around Hard Rock Stadium, Miami became an instant hit with its party atmosphere and entertainment-first approach. The circuit winds through car parks and service roads, featuring a fake marina complete with fake yachts — a very American take on Monaco glamour. Norris won his first-ever F1 race here in 2024, an emotionally charged moment. The high-speed Turn 17 complex and long back straight create genuine overtaking opportunities.
MIA.tyreWear: High — abrasive surface and intense Florida heat degrade tyres rapidly
MIA.keyCorner: Turn 14 (heavy braking, overtaking spot) · Turn 17 (high-speed complex)
MIA.weather: Hot and humid — Florida sun, occasional storm threat
notable: Sprint weekend — 5 Straight Mode zones in 2026

MAD.name: Madrid Street Circuit (IFEMA)
MAD.bio: Madrid's maiden Formula 1 Grand Prix replaces Imola on the 2026 calendar, becoming the second Spanish race alongside Barcelona-Catalunya. The circuit is built around the IFEMA convention and exhibition centre in the east of the capital, with long straights and technical slow corners expected to create overtaking opportunities. Madrid becomes the first new street circuit in Europe since Monaco, and the passionate Spanish crowd — including fans of Fernando Alonso — will create a unique atmosphere for a debut grand prix.
MAD.tyreWear: Unknown — new circuit. Street surface expected to be green initially, evolving dramatically across the weekend
MAD.keyCorner: T1 (heavy braking) · IFEMA complex · Final chicane
notable: Built around IFEMA Madrid exhibition centre

MON.name: Circuit de Monaco
MON.bio: The jewel in Formula 1's crown and the most famous race in motorsport. Monaco has hosted the Grand Prix since 1929 — the circuit has barely changed. The streets of the principality are impossibly narrow; barriers replace run-off; a moment's loss of concentration ends races. Overtaking is nearly impossible, making qualifying the race. Senna won here 6 times. The tunnel, Casino Square, Rascasse and Tabac are corners seared into motorsport mythology.
MON.tyreWear: Very low — slow speeds, cool conditions. Longest race despite shortest lap
MON.keyCorner: Sainte Devote (Turn 1) · Casino Square · Swimming Pool complex · Rascasse
notable: Overtaking almost impossible — qualifying is the race
Narrowest circuit in F1 — mirror-width barriers

BCN.name: Circuit de Barcelona-Catalunya
BCN.bio: Barcelona-Catalunya is the most scrutinised circuit on the calendar — it has been the pre-season testing venue for decades, so every team arrives knowing every bump. In 2026 the Spanish Grand Prix moved to Madrid's new street circuit, but Barcelona-Catalunya continues as a separate race called the Barcelona-Catalunya Grand Prix. The long Turn 3 sweeper and technical final sector demand aero balance. Alonso won here twice to enormous Spanish home support.
BCN.tyreWear: High — abrasive tarmac and sustained high-g loads degrade all four compounds
BCN.keyCorner: Turn 3 (long medium-speed left) · Turn 9 (chicane) · Turn 10 (fast right)
BCN.weather: Hot and sunny — mid-June Mediterranean heat
notable: Pre-season testing venue — teams know it inside out
Now separate from Spanish GP (which moved to Madrid)
Alonso won twice here for home crowd

CAN.name: Circuit Gilles Villeneuve
CAN.bio: Named after the legendary Gilles Villeneuve, this island circuit in the St. Lawrence River is one of the most dramatic on the calendar. Long straights separated by tight chicanes make it a high-power, high-overtaking track. The 'Wall of Champions' — the barrier at the final chicane — has destroyed title bids across the decades. Safety Cars are frequent. Rain often changes the race. Sainz's emotional win here for Williams in 2025 was one of the year's best stories.
CAN.tyreWear: Low — slow chicanes and straights limit sustained cornering loads
CAN.keyCorner: Hairpin (Turn 10) — prime overtaking spot · Wall of Champions (Turn 13–14)
CAN.weather: Unpredictable — can be scorching or raining in June
notable: Wall of Champions claimed Schumacher, Hill, Villeneuve

AUT.name: Red Bull Ring
AUT.bio: The Red Bull Ring sits in the Styrian mountains and is one of F1's shortest but most action-packed circuits. With only 10 corners and 4 estimated Straight Mode zones it's pure power and spectacle — overtakes happen everywhere, aided by Overtake Mode on the main straight. The steep uphill run to Turn 1 and the fast Turns 6-7 complex are immediately recognisable. The circuit is owned by Red Bull, giving it a unique atmosphere. It often hosts two races in back-to-back weekends.
AUT.tyreWear: Low to medium — few sustained high-load corners, cool mountain air helps
AUT.keyCorner: Turn 1 (uphill braking) · Turn 3 (Remus — fast right) · Turn 6–7 (Rindt complex)
AUT.weather: Warm but unpredictable — mountain storms develop quickly
notable: Shortest circuit lap time in F1
Niki Lauda's home GP — huge Austrian support

GBR.name: Silverstone Circuit
GBR.bio: Silverstone hosted the very first World Championship Grand Prix in 1950 and remains the spiritual home of Formula 1. The high-speed Maggotts-Becketts-Chapel complex is the most demanding sequence of corners in motorsport — drivers take six direction changes at over 280 km/h. Copse, Stowe and Club create the classic Silverstone flow. The British crowd is large, knowledgeable and passionate. Hamilton won here a record eight times.
GBR.tyreWear: High — fastest average circuit speed punishes rubber through sustained loads
GBR.keyCorner: Copse (flat in qualifying) · Maggotts-Becketts-Chapel (360 km/h sequence) · Stowe
GBR.weather: Typically British — can change from sunny to wet within a lap
notable: First ever World Championship GP — 13 May 1950
Hamilton won 8 times at his home GP
Maggotts-Becketts complex — fastest sequence in F1

BEL.name: Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps
BEL.bio: Spa-Francorchamps is the longest, fastest and most loved circuit in Formula 1. The Ardennes forest setting, the 104-metre elevation change, and corners like Eau Rouge/Raidillon, Pouhon and Blanchimont are the stuff of motorsport legend. Eau Rouge — once flat out in 2017–2021, then a chicane was added for safety — is the single most iconic corner in F1. Weather changes lap by lap; it can be dry at one end and raining at the other.
BEL.tyreWear: Medium — long straights rest tyres; Pouhon and Raidillon load them hard
BEL.keyCorner: Eau Rouge / Raidillon (legendary uphill complex) · Pouhon (double-apex fast left) · Bus Stop chicane
BEL.weather: Notoriously unpredictable — fog, rain, sun all possible in the same race
notable: Eau Rouge — most iconic corner in motorsport
Rain is expected almost every year

HUN.name: Hungaroring
HUN.bio: The Hungaroring is often compared to Monaco without the barriers — tight, twisty, low-speed and almost impossible to overtake on. It demands mechanical grip and tyre management above everything. The summer heat pushes track temperatures above 50°C, destroying rubber. Yet races here are rarely dull — strategy and chaos collide. Ocon's stunning win in 2021 came after Hamilton accidentally swallowed a magnet at lunch. Piastri's first victory came here in 2024.
HUN.tyreWear: Very high — extreme summer temperatures and sustained cornering loads are punishing
HUN.keyCorner: Turn 1 (overtaking opportunity) · Turn 4 (long medium-speed right) · Turn 11 (hairpin)
HUN.weather: Extremely hot — mid-summer Budapest, track temperatures exceed 50°C
notable: First GP behind Iron Curtain in 1986
Effectively Monaco without barriers — minimal overtaking

NED.name: Circuit Zandvoort
NED.bio: Zandvoort returned to the calendar in 2021 after a 36-year absence, transformed with spectacular banked corners at Turns 3 and 14 that make it unlike anywhere else on the calendar. The circuit sits in dunes next to the North Sea. Verstappen's home race generates an extraordinary atmosphere — the entire grandstand is orange. **2026 is the final Dutch GP on the current calendar** as Zandvoort's contract expires. Fittingly, it will host its first-ever Sprint weekend, making the farewell even more special.
NED.tyreWear: Medium — banking reduces lateral tyre stress; damp sea air helps temperatures
NED.keyCorner: Hugenholt (T3 — banked corner) · T14 (banked hairpin) · Arie Luyendyk (T7)
NED.weather: Coastal — sea breeze keeps it cooler; afternoon showers common
notable: FINAL Dutch GP — Zandvoort contract expires after 2026
Banked corners unique in modern F1

ITA.name: Autodromo Nazionale di Monza
ITA.bio: Monza is the fastest circuit in Formula 1 — the Temple of Speed. Teams strip aerodynamic downforce to the bone here, running the lowest drag configurations of the season. The long straights and three chicanes create a brutal back-and-forth rhythm. The Tifosi — Ferrari's fanatical Italian fans — fill the grandstands orange and red every September. Gasly's miracle win here in 2020 for AlphaTauri was one of the greatest upsets in F1 history.
ITA.tyreWear: Very low — minimal sustained cornering. Engine and brakes take the stress
ITA.keyCorner: T1 chicane (braking from 363 km/h) · Lesmo 1–2 · Parabolica (final corner)
ITA.weather: Late summer — warm and usually dry, can have sudden storms
notable: Fastest average speed circuit in F1
Tifosi — most passionate crowd in motorsport
Teams run minimum downforce — closest to dragster specification

AZE.name: Baku City Circuit
AZE.bio: Baku's city circuit is F1's most unpredictable race venue. The 2.2km straight down to Turn 1 is the longest in Formula 1 — top speeds exceed 368 km/h — yet drivers must then navigate medieval castle walls with only centimetres to spare. Safety Cars are almost guaranteed. The 2026 Azerbaijan GP is notable for being held on a **Saturday** (not Sunday) following a request from the promoter to accommodate Azerbaijan's national Remembrance Day — a unique scheduling quirk in the calendar.
AZE.tyreWear: High on brakes and front tyres — extreme braking from 368 km/h; rear tyres rest on the straight
AZE.keyCorner: T1 (braking from 368 km/h — longest braking zone in F1) · Castle section (2m wide — walls everywhere)
AZE.weather: Late September — warm and usually dry
notable: Narrowest section (castle walls) anywhere in F1
Safety Car almost guaranteed every year
2026 race held on a SATURDAY (Remembrance Day)

SGP.name: Marina Bay Street Circuit
SGP.bio: Marina Bay is the only night race in Formula 1. Under 1,500 floodlights, the circuit snakes through the financial district, past the iconic Esplanade theatre and over the Anderson Bridge. It is the slowest average speed circuit on the calendar and demands the most physical effort from drivers — temperatures of 34°C and near-100% humidity turn the cockpit into a sauna. Safety Cars are frequent. **2026 is Singapore's first Sprint weekend**, adding extra drama to an already unpredictable event.
SGP.tyreWear: High — bumpy surface and relentless cornering in extreme heat
SGP.keyCorner: T5 (heavy braking) · T10 (Turn into the tunnel) · T13 (Anderson Bridge) · T23 (Marina Bay hairpin)
SGP.weather: Tropical night — 34°C, 90%+ humidity. Rain is possible mid-race
notable: Slowest average speed on calendar (171 km/h)
Most physically demanding race — 2-hour cockpit sauna

USA.name: Circuit of the Americas
USA.bio: COTA is American-built but European-inspired. Hermann Tilke designed it to incorporate elements of the world's greatest circuits — the Turn 1 uphill blind entry is inspired by Hockenheim; the Esses echo Maggotts-Becketts; the hairpin mirrors Istanbul. The result is a circuit that works on multiple levels. The passionate Texas crowd and incredible Austin atmosphere make race weekend one of F1's most entertaining. Sprint race weekend. Verstappen has dominated here.
USA.tyreWear: High — medium-speed corners and bumpy surface combine to stress all four compounds
USA.keyCorner: T1 (blind uphill entry — cars touch 0g) · T12 (high-speed hairpin) · T16–T18 (fast complex)
USA.weather: October heat — 30°C+ but cooling evening. Rain possible
notable: Designed to blend world's great circuits
Bumps at Turn 9 cause significant car movement
Texas crowd creates unique F1 atmosphere

MEX.name: Autodromo Hermanos Rodriguez
MEX.bio: Mexico City sits at 2,240 metres above sea level — the highest circuit on the F1 calendar. Thin air means engines produce less power and aerodynamic downforce is dramatically reduced; teams run maximum wing angles but still generate less grip than at sea level. Brakes must work in the thin air. The atmosphere inside the stadium section — where 50,000 fans pack into an outdoor concert setting around the final hairpin — is unmatched anywhere in sport.
MEX.tyreWear: Low — thin air, smooth tarmac. Brakes suffer more than tyres
MEX.keyCorner: T1 (braking from 370 km/h — longest brake zone in F1) · Stadium section (T17 hairpin)
MEX.weather: Warm days, cool evenings at altitude — ideal racing
notable: Stadium section crowd creates concert atmosphere

BRA.name: Autodromo Jose Carlos Pace (Interlagos)
BRA.bio: Interlagos is one of Formula 1's most atmospheric venues — the anti-clockwise layout, the packed grandstands, the São Paulo altitude and the history all combine into something unique. Senna's home race. The Curva do Sol and Junção corners are taken at incredible speed. Drama is guaranteed; rain is frequent. Hamilton's miracle 2008 title win was decided on the final corner of the final lap here. Sprint race weekend. Bortoleto has an enormous home crowd to power him.
BRA.tyreWear: High — anti-clockwise layout loads left-front tyre; altitude affects brake cooling
BRA.keyCorner: Senna S (T1–T2 complex) · Curva do Sol (T4 — fast left) · Junção (T10)
BRA.weather: Tropical — hot with afternoon thunderstorms almost certain
notable: Hamilton 2008 title decided last corner last lap

LVS.name: Las Vegas Strip Circuit
LVS.bio: Formula 1 returned to Las Vegas in 2023 for a night race down the Strip — past the MGM Grand, the Bellagio fountains and the Sphere. At 6.2km it is one of the longest street circuits. The long straights and five estimated Straight Mode zones (available to all drivers) make it a high-speed spectacle but the cool November desert temperatures mean tyre warm-up is critical. Safety Cars are common and the midnight start time gives it an atmosphere unlike anything else in sport.
LVS.tyreWear: High initially — desert cold makes tyres difficult to warm; abrasive new surface
LVS.keyCorner: T1 (braking from 345 km/h) · Fountain section (T12–T14) · T17 hairpin (overtaking spot)
LVS.weather: Cool desert night — November temperatures around 15–20°C despite Las Vegas heat in summer
notable: Night race down the Las Vegas Strip
Cool desert temperatures — opposite of summer Vegas

QAT.name: Lusail International Circuit
QAT.bio: Lusail is a high-speed technical circuit with flowing medium-to-fast corners that load tyres relentlessly. Originally built for MotoGP, its narrow layout and minimal run-off create an intense challenge. The extreme heat — track temperatures above 60°C at night — and the relentless rhythm of corners destroys rear tyres fast; the 2023 race saw multiple retirements due to tyre failures. Sprint race weekend. Qatar's growing F1 presence signals major financial investment in the sport.
QAT.tyreWear: Extreme — high-speed cornering at 50–60°C track temperature destroys tyres. Multiple failures in 2023
QAT.keyCorner: T1 (high-speed right) · T6–T8 (flowing medium-speed esses) · T14 (hairpin — overtaking)
QAT.weather: Hot desert night — 35°C air, 60°C+ track. Dusty conditions can affect grip
notable: 2023 race — multiple tyre failures forced retirements
Originally a MotoGP venue — narrow and technical

ABU.name: Yas Marina Circuit
ABU.bio: Yas Marina closes the Formula 1 season every year with its spectacular sunset-to-night race. The circuit was redesigned in 2021 to eliminate the slow section and now flows much better. Four estimated Straight Mode zones (open to all drivers for efficiency) plus Overtake Mode on the main straight make overtaking possible. The hotel tower visible through the circuit, the yacht-lined marina and the final spectacular hairpin create the perfect backdrop for a season finale. The 2021 finale — Hamilton vs Verstappen decided in controversy with three laps to go — was one of sport's most dramatic moments.
ABU.tyreWear: Medium — smooth tarmac and Straight Mode zones provide rest; medium-speed corners load evenly
ABU.keyCorner: T5 (hairpin — Overtake Mode zone) · T9 (hotel section) · T21 (final hairpin — season-ending corner)
ABU.weather: Warm desert evening — 28°C with a desert breeze. Sunset to night race
notable: Season finale — championship regularly decided here
Sunset to night race — spectacular lighting
2021 finale — Hamilton vs Verstappen — controversial Safety Car
